description: Specialized in shipbuilding, boat repair, and maritime navigation
materialRequirements: Wood for shipyard structures, access to deep water

tieredBenefits.description: 10% faster sailing, better wind handling, basic ship repair
20% faster sailing, can tack at wider angles
30% faster sailing, excellent wind handling, smaller turning radius

randomTalk: The sea is a harsh mistress. Well, I say 'mistress.' More of a salty wench who occasionally tries to murder you.
I've been building boats for twenty years. The trick is knowing which end goes in the water. You'd be SURPRISED how many people get that wrong.
Serpents? Oh, serpents are fine. It's the bit between you and the serpent I worry about. That's the water. And I've MENTIONED the water.
My father was a shipwright. His father was a shipwright. His father was a duck. We don't talk about Sven.
People say the sea is calling them. The sea isn't calling anyone. The sea is ignoring you very loudly.
I named my last ship 'Nigh Unsinkable.' Before that I named one 'Surely Unsinkable.' I'm running out of adjectives.
Port. Starboard. Bow. Stern. Four words. If you need more than four words, you're overcomplicating it.
The Carpenter thinks wood is about joinery. Wood is about TRUST. You have to trust the plank. If the plank doesn't want to be a boat, you can't MAKE it be a boat.
A boat is just a hole in the water you pour money into. I'm the one holding the bucket.
They say a captain goes down with his ship. As a shipwright, my responsibility ends at the pier.
I waterproofed a longship so well once, it leaked on the OUTSIDE. Still not sure how it happened.
Stop touching the hull! ...Sorry. Reflex. Last person who touched a hull without asking lost a finger. Not to me. To the hull.
I talk to the wood. The wood talks back. If it screams, that's a bad plank.
There's a saying: 'Red sky at night, sailor's delight.' There's another saying: 'Red sky in morning, serpent swallowed the sun.' Both equally useful.
Every plank I nail is a promise to someone that they won't drown. No pressure. Literally. Don't pressure the planks.
randomGreets: Ahoy! Mind the— too late. That's tar. That's tar forever now.
Welcome to the shipyard! Touch nothing, admire everything.
Oh good, someone who ISN'T on fire. Come in, come in.
You! You look like you've got all your fingers. Interested in keeping them? Stay away from the saw.
Greetings! You here for a boat or just wandering? We get a lot of wanderers. Most leave with boats. Funny how that works.
Hello! Are those dry boots? Enjoy that while it lasts.
randomGoodbye: Fair winds! Or at minimum, winds that don't cheat very much.
Off you go then. Try not to drown on land. It IS possible. I've seen it.
Smooth sailing! That's a wish, not a guarantee. I provide boats, not weather.
May your keel stay straight and your mast stay upright! Beyond that, you're on your own.
Come back when you need a boat! Come back BEFORE you need a boat, really. Emergencies cost extra.
